dailyJudgementSnippet: OpenAI won the day mainly by being the only tracked provider with net-favorable direct mentions: Codex/GPT 5.3 got modest praise in coding-tool comparisons, while the biggest AI conversation centered on backlash to Claude Code’s UX and product direction rather than OpenAI missteps [E5][E6][E1][E3].
winnerExplanation: OpenAI finished first because Hacker News gave it a small but real positive signal across two coding-related threads: one commenter said recent Codex 5.3 improvements pulled substantial usage back to it, and another still treated GPT 5.3 as the model to keep around for detailed analysis even in a mixed comparison [E5][E6]. That was enough to beat a day where Anthropic had far more attention but much of it was critical of Claude Code’s changes, reliability, and trustworthiness [E1][E3].

openai.judgementSnippet: Codex/GPT 5.3 picked up modest goodwill in coding discussions: one heavy user said recent Codex improvements brought them back to it in a big way, and a GLM-5 comparison still framed GPT 5.3 as useful for detailed analysis even while criticizing some prior output [E5][E6].

anthropic.judgementSnippet: Claude Code drew the day’s biggest AI backlash over hidden output, breakage, and a sense that Anthropic was steering the product away from power users; a separate Show HN thread reinforced skepticism about Claude Code handling hard OOXML edge cases, and only a smaller user testimonial about fast cross-platform app work meaningfully pushed the other way [E1][E3][E2][E4].

evidence.summary: The front-page Claude Code backlash thread was dominated by complaints that Anthropic hid useful agent output, shipped breaking/performance regressions, and was making product-direction choices power users disliked; Anthropic staff acknowledged they missed the mark for some users.
In the Fluorite thread, a user said the combination of Flutter and Claude Code made cross-platform development 'really, really fast' and praised Claude for handling nuanced platform-specific prompts.
Show HN comments about a DOCX editor built with Claude Code were skeptical that Claude could handle OOXML edge cases, warning about hallucinations, missing features, and demo-only completeness.

In the GLM-5 discussion, commenters used Claude/Opus/Haiku as a quality reference point, saying Opus 4.5 was a clear jump and that GLM's reasoning traces felt 'like Claude,' but without a strong net push beyond benchmark-style comparison.

Inside the Claude Code backlash thread, one heavy user said they were 'very happy' with recent Codex 5.3 improvements, were spending 'A LOT' more time with Codex, and liked that its agent toolchain was open source.

GLM-5 comparisons were mixed for GPT 5.3/Codex: one commenter said GLM cleaned up work Codex had left behind, but also said GPT 5.3 was still the tool for especially nitty-gritty analysis and worth pairing with another model.
